content: The welding process commonly used for fabricating tailor-welded blanks of dissimilar thickness for automotive applications is
explanation:
The process most widely used for tailor-welded blanks in the auto industry is laser welding. Its advantages include:

Very narrow heat-affected zone (HAZ), so you get minimal distortion.

High welding speed, which is essential for production volume.

Excellent weld‐bead appearance and consistency—critical when blanks go straight into stamping dies.

Answer: Option B – laser welding.

content: The most suitable electrode material used for joining low alloy steels using Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) process is
explanation:
In Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW), the selection of the electrode material is critical for achieving a strong and high-quality weld. When welding low alloy steels, the ideal electrode material should have similar properties to the base material to ensure compatibility and prevent issues such as cracking or poor weld quality.

Considering the options:

Option A: Copper

Copper is generally not used as an electrode material for welding low alloy steels because it does not have compatible mechanical properties and could lead to undesirable outcomes in the weld.

Option B: Cadmium

Cadmium is toxic and is not used in welding processes, especially in GMAW, as an electrode material for low alloy steels or any other materials.

Option C: Low alloy steel

This is the most suitable electrode material for joining low alloy steels using the GMAW process. The use of low alloy steel electrodes ensures that the chemical composition and mechanical properties of the weld deposit are similar to those of the base material, resulting in a high-quality weld.

Option D: Tungsten

Tungsten electrodes are primarily used in Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) or TIG welding, not in GMAW for welding low alloy steels. Tungsten's properties do not match those of low alloy steels and are not optimal for this application.

Therefore, the most suitable electrode material used for joining low alloy steels using the GMAW process is:

Option C: Low alloy steel

content: Two surfaces P and Q are to be joined together. In which of the given joining operation(s), there is no melting of the two surfaces P and Q for creating the joint?
explanation:
When determining which joining operations do not involve melting the surfaces being joined, it's helpful to examine each option in detail:

Option A - Arc Welding: This method uses an electric arc to melt the metals at the joining point. Hence, both surfaces will experience melting.

Option B - Brazing: Brazing involves melting a filler metal, which has a lower melting point than the pieces being joined. The surfaces of P and Q are not melted; only the filler metal is. So, this method does not involve the melting of P and Q.

Option C - Adhesive Bonding: This technique uses an adhesive substance to bond surfaces together without melting them. Therefore, there is no melting of surfaces P and Q.

Option D - Spot Welding: Spot welding is a resistance welding process that involves the localized melting of the surfaces to be joined. Thus, surfaces P and Q are melted during this process.

Therefore, the correct joining operations where there is no melting of the two surfaces P and Q are:

Option B: Brazing

Option C: Adhesive bonding

content: Which of the following heat treatment processes is/are used for surface hardening of steels?
explanation:
Surface hardening of steels involves various heat treatment processes that are typically employed to improve the wear resistance of the surface while maintaining a tougher and more ductile interior. Several processes are used for this purpose, and they include the following options:

Option A: Carburizing

Carburizing is a surface hardening process where the steel is exposed to a carbon-rich environment at high temperatures. The carbon diffuses into the surface, creating a high-carbon layer that can be hardened through quenching.

Option B: Cyaniding

Cyaniding is another surface hardening method where the steel is heated in the presence of a cyanide-based compound, which introduces both carbon and nitrogen into the surface. This process is quick and results in a hard surface layer.

Option C: Annealing

Annealing is not a surface hardening process. Instead, it is used to soften the steel, relieve internal stresses, and improve its ductility. During annealing, the steel is heated to a high temperature and then allowed to cool slowly.

Option D: Carbonitriding

Carbonitriding is similar to carburizing but involves introducing both carbon and nitrogen into the surface layer. This process is typically conducted at a lower temperature than carburizing and results in a hard, wear-resistant surface.

Based on the above explanations, the heat treatment processes used for surface hardening of steels are:

  • Option A: Carburizing
  • Option B: Cyaniding
  • Option D: Carbonitriding

Therefore, Options A, B, and D are correct for surface hardening of steels.

content: Which of the following additive manufacturing technique(s) can use a wire as a feedstock material?
explanation:
The correct answer is Option B: Fused deposition modeling (FDM) and Option D: Directed energy deposition processes (DED).

Here's a breakdown of why:

Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM):

FDM is a widely used additive manufacturing technique that uses a thermoplastic filament as a feedstock. This filament is typically spooled on a reel and fed through a heated nozzle. The nozzle melts the filament, which is then deposited layer by layer to build up the 3D object. Wire feedstock is often used in FDM for larger-scale printing. It provides a more cost-effective alternative to using filaments, especially for materials like metal alloys.

Directed Energy Deposition (DED):

DED is a category of additive manufacturing processes that involves melting and depositing material onto a substrate using a focused energy source (e.g., a laser or electron beam). One of the common variations within DED is wire arc additive manufacturing (WAAM), which utilizes a wire feedstock. In WAAM, a wire is fed into the welding torch, where it is melted by an arc, and then deposited onto the substrate, layer by layer.

Why the other options are incorrect:

Stereolithography (SLA): SLA uses a vat of liquid photopolymer resin as its feedstock. A UV laser selectively cures the resin, solidifying it layer by layer. It does not use wire as a feedstock.

Selective Laser Sintering (SLS): SLS utilizes a powder bed of thermoplastic or metal powder as its feedstock. A laser selectively melts and fuses the powder particles to form the desired object. It does not use wire as a feedstock.

In summary, FDM and DED are the additive manufacturing techniques that can utilize wire as a feedstock material. FDM is typically used for thermoplastic materials, while DED processes like WAAM can be used for metal alloys and other materials.

content: At small variations of are length at operating conditions, the manual metal are welding transformer provides nearly

In an arc welding process, the voltage and current are 30 V and 200 A, respectively. The cross-sectional area of the joint is 20 mm2 and the welding speed is 5 mm/s. The heat required to melt the material is 20 J/s. The percentage of heat lost to the surrounding during the welding process is _______ (rounded off to 2 decimal places).

In a direct current arc welding process, the power source has an open circuit voltage of 100 V and short circuit current of 1000 A. Assume a linear relationship between voltage and current. The arc voltage (V) varies with the arc length (l) as V = 10 + 5l where V is in volts and l is in mm. The maximum available arc power during the process is ______ kVA (in integer).
explanation:
Open Circuit Voltage :

$ V_{OC} = 100 \, \text{V} $

Short Circuit Current :

$ I_{SC} = 1000 \, \text{A} $

Given :

$ V = 10 + 5l $

For maximum power :

$ \begin{align*} V_{Arc} &= \frac{V_{OC}}{2} \\ I_{Arc} &= \frac{I_{SC}}{2} \end{align*} $

So, the Maximum Power $P$,

$ \begin{align*} P &= V_{Arc} \times I_{Arc} \\\\ &= \frac{V_{OC}}{2} \times \frac{I_{SC}}{2} \\\\ &= \frac{100}{2} \times \frac{1000}{2} \\\\ &= 50 \times 500 \\\\ &= 25000 \, \text{VA} \\\\ &= 25 \, \text{kVA} \end{align*} $

Hence, the maximum available power during the process is 25 kVA.

content:
Two mild steel plates of similar thickness, in butt-joint configuration, are welded by gas tungsten arc welding process using the following welding parameters.

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text { Welding voltage } & 20 \mathrm{~V} \\ \hline \text { Welding current } & 150 \mathrm{~A} \\ \hline \text { Welding speed } & 5 \mathrm{~mm} / \mathrm{s} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

A filler wire of the same mild steel material having 3 mm diameter is used in this welding process. The filler wire feed rate is selected such that the final weld bead is composed of 60% volume of filler and 40% volume of plate material. The heat required to melt the mild steel material is 10 J/mm3. The heat transfer factor is 0.7 and melting factor is 0.6. The feed rate of the filler wire is __________ mm/s (round off to one decimal place).
explanation:
Given :

Voltage ($V$) = 20 V

Current ($I$) = 150 A

Welding speed ($V_s$) = 5 mm/s

Diameter of filler wire ($d$) = 3 mm

Heat required to melt mild steel material ($Q$) = 10 J/mm³

Heat transfer efficiency ($\eta_{HT}$) = 0.7

Melting efficiency ($\eta_m$) = 0.6

Let the feed rate of the filler wire be $ f $.

Calculations :

Input energy :

$ P_{in} = V \times I $

$ = 150 \times 20 = 3000 $ J/s

Available energy :

$ \text{Available energy} = \eta_{HT} \times \eta_m \times P_{in} $

$ = 0.7 \times 0.6 \times 3000 = 1260 $ J/s

Rate of deposition of filler material :

$ \text{Rate of deposition of filler material} = \text{Cross-sectional Area of filler} \times f $

$ = \frac{\pi}{4} \times d^2 \times f $

Rate of welding at which bead is formed :

$ = \text{Rate of deposition of filler material} $

Therefore,

$ A_\omega \times V_s \times 0.6 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times d^2 \times f $

Heat required :

$ Q = \frac{\text{Available energy}}{\text{Area of weld} \times \text{Welding speed}} $

$ 10 = \frac{1260}{A_\omega \times 5} $

$ A_\omega = 25.2 $ mm²

Substituting $ A_\omega $ back into the equation :

$ 25.2 \times 5 \times 0.6 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 3^2 \times f $

Solving for the feed rate $ f $, we get :

$ f = 10.695 $ mm/s

Conclusion :

The feed rate of the filler wire is 10.7 mm/s (rounded to one decimal place).
